item.title: Socialist Soccer! 2pm - 4pm
item.description:
Sunday, April 12, 2026 at 2 PM

Location: Brighton Playfield\, 6000 39th Ave S\, Seattle\, WA\, 98118\, United States

Come play soccer with fellow socialists! This is a fun\, enjoyable way to get outdoors\, exercise\, and meet friendly socialists as well as non-socialist soccer enthusiasts! All genders and skill levels are welcome! Safety is our top priority. To participate you must abide by our norms and agreements to avoid injuries and help us take care of one another. Join us at Brighton Playfield in South Seattle. Map: goo.gl/maps/c74cytjrRB1vpT7h7 We almost always play on the most southern edge of the field. We confirm if we have enough players each Sunday on a socialist soccer Signal chat. To be added\, download the Signal app\, and email your phone number to tdupuis42@gmail.com with a request to be added. The game is usually at 10 AM\, but that may change\, so make sure you are on the Socialist Soccer signal chat!

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Monday, April 13, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Sign up for membership to take advantage of waived initiation fees. Don’t miss this chance to test out our space, meet amazing people, and see if Cloud Room is the perfect fit for your work needs! item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Monday, April 13, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

item.description:
Tuesday, April 14, 2026 at 9:30 AM

Location: Bothell Kenmore Chamber, 10031 Main Street, Suite A, Bothell, WA

Tuesday Cowork \+ Coffee/Tea \(Weekly\) Need a reason to get out of your inbox cave and into a productive, low-pressure space? Join us every Tuesday for a drop-in coworking morning—bring your laptop, knock out your priorities, and enjoy the energy of working alongside other local professionals. When: Tuesdays | 9:30–11:30 AM | No registration needed Format: Come for the full two hours or pop in when you can Who it’s for: Business owners, remote workers, creatives, students, nonprofit leaders—anyone who wants focused work time \+ light community What to expect: Quiet “get-it-done” work blocks, optional hellos \+ quick intros, and a friendly vibe \(no forced networking\) Location \(through February\): Bothell Visitor Center We’ll rotate locations monthly—same time, same Tuesday rhythm, fresh spaces. Bring: Your laptop \+ whatever you need to work. We have the coffee, tea, and water! Questions? Email sandra@bothellkenmorechamber.org

item.title: Moomins' Sea Adventures & Tove and the Sea
item.description:
Tuesday, April 14, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: National Nordic Museum, 2655 NW Market Street, Seattle, WA

Dive into the whimsical world of Moominvalley and the life of its creator, Tove Jansson, with *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* and *Tove and the Sea*. *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* brings Moominvalley to life with illustrations and life-sized characters from the stories. In the center of the exhibit is an interactive island complete with a lighthouse and rowboats that invites visitors to imagine adventures of their own. *Tove and the Sea* offers a more intimate counterpoint, tracing Tove Jansson’s lifelong relationship with the Finnish archipelago through large-scale photographs and reflective texts.

item.title: Low Vision Book Group: "West with Giraffes"
item.description:
Tuesday, April 14, 2026 at 11:30 AM

Tuesday, April 14, 2026, 11:30am - 1pm Join our Low Vision Book Club for a discussion of "West with Giraffes" by Lynda Rutledge. Location Link: Central Library Room Location: Level 4 - Room 6 Registration Required: Registration is not required. Detailed Description: About the book:, Woodrow Wilson Nickel, age 105, feels his life ebbing away. But when he learns giraffes are going extinct, he finds himself recalling the unforgettable experience he cannot take to his grave. It's 1938. The Great Depression lingers. Hitler is threatening Europe, and world-weary Americans long for wonder. They find it in two giraffes who miraculously survive a hurricane while crossing the Atlantic. What follows is a twelve-day road trip in a custom truck to deliver Southern California's first giraffes to the San Diego Zoo. Behind the wheel is the young Dust Bowl rowdy Woodrow. Inspired by true events, the tale weaves real-life figures with fictional ones, including the world's first female zoo director, a crusty old man with a past, a young female photographer with a secret, and assorted reprobates as spotty as the giraffes. Part adventure, part historical saga, and part coming-of-age love story, West with Giraffes explores what it means to be changed by the grace of animals, the kindness of strangers, the passing of time, and a story told before it's too late. Event Logistics: View in catalog: " West With Giraffes " byLyndaRutledge. Contact Us: If you have questions about this event, Ask Us or call 206-386-4636. Please contactLEAP at least seven days before the event to request accommodations., All Library programs are free and open to the public.

item.title: Local Council Meeting
item.description:
Tuesday, April 14, 2026 at 7 PM

Location: Online (Discord)

The Local Council (LC) is the elected leadership body which serves the members of Seattle DSA. During these short weekly check-ins\, LC members will primarily be ensuring work is on track\, though pressing business may occasionally be discussed and voted on. Members are welcome to observe LC meetings\, which are held on the chapter’s Discord server. To join our Discord\, please visit https://seattledsa.org/join-discord/ and follow the instructions. Thereafter\, you will be able to observe LC meetings on the #lc-voice channel every Tuesday (except the fourth Tuesday of the month when we hold our monthly General Membership Meeting) from 7:00pm – 7:45pm over Zoom. \nIf there’s something specific you’d like to bring to the Local Council\, please fill out the LC Request Form which can be found on the private resources page of our website. \nPlease note that on rare occurrences\, LC meetings will be cancelled due to holidays or unusual circumstances and these changes may not always be reflected on this calendar — if you show up to the meeting and aren’t let in after 5 minutes\, that is almost certainly what has happened.

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

item.title: Coworking Open House Week @ The Cloud Room
item.description:
Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 9 AM

Location: The Cloud Room, 1424 11th Ave Suite 400, Seattle, WA

Curious about coworking and looking for the perfect spot to get work done and reach your goals? Join us for a complimentary day during our upcoming Open House Week and experience what it’s like to be a part of our incredible community! What's Included: One Free Trial Day: Pick any day during Open House Week to try out our coworking space, free of charge! Arrive anytime 9am or later — whatever works best with your schedule. Access to our beautiful workspace including quiet desk space with monitors, large lounge area, phone booths, bar, and outdoor patio. Complimentary coffee, tea, snacks and kombucha. High-speed Wi-Fi, printing, and meeting rooms available for rent The chance to meet like-minded professionals and get a feel for the Cloud Room vibe. Special Offer: If you love your free day with us and decide to become a member, we’ll waive your initiation fees when you sign up! How to Join: Reserve your free trial day by selecting one day of your choice on our Calendly \(one free trial day per person\) Show up and enjoy your day at Cloud Room! Be sure to check in with the staff at the front of the lounge so we can give you a quick tour of the space and get you set up with the wifi. Sign up for membership to take advantage of waived initiation fees. Don’t miss this chance to test out our space, meet amazing people, and see if Cloud Room is the perfect fit for your work needs! Current Promotions: ⭐️ Sign up for a grounded membership and get your first month free \+ 10% off you first year of membership. 3 month commitment required. ⭐️ Floating Membership Promo: Take 50% off your first full month of floating membership. Spaces are limited—RSVP today.

item.title: Moomins' Sea Adventures & Tove and the Sea
item.description:
Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: National Nordic Museum, 2655 NW Market Street, Seattle, WA

Dive into the whimsical world of Moominvalley and the life of its creator, Tove Jansson, with *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* and *Tove and the Sea*. *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* brings Moominvalley to life with illustrations and life-sized characters from the stories. In the center of the exhibit is an interactive island complete with a lighthouse and rowboats that invites visitors to imagine adventures of their own. *Tove and the Sea* offers a more intimate counterpoint, tracing Tove Jansson’s lifelong relationship with the Finnish archipelago through large-scale photographs and reflective texts.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: You've Had It In You All Along
item.description:
Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Avery Barnes Gallery, 120 First Ave. S., Seattle, WA

Artists: Barbara Earl Thomas | Tariqa Waters | Cristina Martinez | Robert Wade | Maikoiyo Alley-Barnes | Nahom Ghirmay “ You’ve Had It In You All Along” is an affirmation of our capability to weather any storm. It serves as a vital reminder that the tools required to reach your destination are already etched within your spirit. We already carry the brilliance and the beauty of what it takes—the confidence, the strength, the security, the poise, and the knowledge to achieve our goals. The grit, the rigor, the radical ideas, and the voice required to transcend the world around us are not external gifts to be acquired; they are inherent powers waiting for the moment of their activation.  In the process of overcoming—when the pathway forward is unclear and the effort feels endless—it is easy to lose sight of our own internal compass. When we stand face-to-face with rejection, heartbreak, or the heavy weight of difficult seasons, this exhibition serves as a sanctuary to remind us that we have always been more than enough, exactly as we are. Breakthroughs happen in the space where we are gentle with ourselves, allowing for the activation of an inner wisdom and intuition that has been present since our first breath.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Người dạy là một giảng viên giàu kinh nghiệm, giảng dạy bằng cả tiếng Việt và tiếng Anh., Lớp học này được tạo ra dành cho những người cần giúp đỡ để chuẩn bị cho bài thi quốc tịch của họ.; lớp được cung cấp bởi tổ chức Asian Counseling and Referral Service ., ---------------------------------------, The class meets Tuesdays and Thursdays for 11 weeks, starting on April 7 and ending on June 18. It is taught by an experienced instructor in Vietnamese and English., This class is designed for individuals that need assistance in preparing for their citizenship exam; the class is provided by Asian Counseling and Referral Service., Thanks to our partner Asian Counseling and Referral Service . Event Logistics: Bắt buộc phải đăng ký để tham gia. Hãy bấm vào đây để đăng ký.Học viên phải nộp bản sao thẻ xanh để ghi danh theo học lớp này., Để tham gia vào lớp học này, quý vị cần có một kết nối mạng internet và một thiết bị để vào mạng internet \(điện thoại thông minh, máy laptop, máy tính bàn, hoặc máy tính bảng\) với phần mềm gọi họp qua mạng Microsoft Teams được cài đặt sẵn, và quý vị sẽ cần một địa chỉ email để đăng ký tham gia., Nếu quý vị cần trợ giúp trong việc tạo một địa chỉ email hoặc cài đặt phần mềm Microsoft Teams, xin liên lạc với bộ phận trợ giúp của tổ chức TechConnect Washington Community trước khi lớp học bắt đầu. Trợ giúp về công nghệ có sẵn trong các ngôn ngữ: tiếng Afaan Oromoo, tiếng Amharic, tiếng Anh, tiếng Sidamo, tiếng Tây Ban Nha, tiếng Tigrinya, tiếng Việt, và tiếng Wolaytta, 7 ngày trong một tuần từ 8h sáng đến 8h tối bằng điện thoại 1-800-216-1132 , đối thoại qua mạng \(web chat\), và qua email tại địa chỉ techconnect@eec-wa.org ., Tìm hiểu thêm về Hỗ trợ công dân. , ---------------------------------------, Registration is required.Register here ., Students are required to submit a copy of their green card in order to register for this class., , To take part in the class you will need an internet connection and a device to connect to the internet \(smartphone, laptop, desktop, or tablet computer\) with the Microsoft Teams web/video conferencing platform installed, and you will need an email address to register., If you need help getting an email address or installing or using Microsoft Teams, contact the TechConnect Washington Community helpdesk before the class is scheduled to begin., Technical support is available in Afaan Oromoo, Amharic, English, Sidamo, Spanish, Tigrinya, Vietnamese, and Wolaytta, 7 days a week between 8 am - 8 pm by phone \(800\) 216-1132 , web chat , and email to techconnect@eec-wa.org ., Find out more about Citizenship Help . Contact Us: Nếu quý vị có thắc mắc về lớp học, hãy liên hệ với giảng viên Duy Ngo qua email duyn@acrs.org hoặc gọi \(206\) 705-8186.---------------------------------------, If you have questions about the class, contact the instructor Duy Ngo via email at duyn@acrs.org , call or textmessage \(206\) 705-8186. ADA Accommodations: Chúng tôi có thể cung cấp hỗ trợ cho những người cần trợ giúp khuyết tật tại các chương trình của Thư Viện. Xin vui lòng liên hệ với LEAP để yêu cầu trợ giúp ít nhất bảy ngày trước khi chương trình diễn ra., Tất cả các chương trình của Thư viện đều miễn phí và mở cửa cho công chúng.---------------------------------------, We can provide accommodations for people with disabilities at Library events. Please contactLEAP at least seven days before the event to request accommodations., All Library programs are free and open to the public.,

item.title: Living with Conviction: Sentenced to Debt for Life in Washington State Exhibit
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Northwest Worklofts (Commons), 107 West Denny Way, Seattle, WA

Thursday, April 16, 2026, 10am - 6pm This exhibitconfronts how Washington courts have been sentencing people not only to prison but also to a lifetime of debt. Location Link: Central Library Room Location: Level 8 - Gallery Registration Required: Registration is not required. Detailed Description: "Living with Conviction: Sentenced to Debt for Life in Washington State" confronts how Washington courts have been sentencing people not only to prison but also to a lifetime of debt. The project leverages multimedia storytelling and community conversations to raise awareness about and advocate for an end to crippling court-imposed costs, fees, fines, and restitution, commonly referred to as Legal Financial Obligations, or LFOs. "Living with Conviction: Sentenced to Debt for Life in Washington State," goes beyond polarizing headlines and statistics, shining a light on this injustice by sharing stories of our common humanity., Thanks to our partner Living with Conviction ., Thanks to our sponsor The Seattle Public Library Foundation . Event Logistics: The gallery is open during Central Libray's open hours., Living with Conviction is a 501\(c\)\(3\) nonprofit that works to secure economic and racial justice with and by marginalized communities, especially formerly incarcerated, through community-driven research, storytelling, and legal empowerment strategies., This exhibit runs through July 26, 2026, Learn more about Exhibits . item.title: Eric-Paul Riege: ojo|-|ólǫ́
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

ojo|-|ólǫ́ is an exhibition of recent and newly commissioned work by Diné artist Eric-Paul Riege \(b. 1994, Na’nízhoozhí \[Gallup, New Mexico\]\) that includes sculpture, textile, collage, and video, activated by moments of performance. Across this work, Riege combines customary Diné practices of weaving, silversmithing, and beading with contemporary cultural forms, exploring Diné cosmology, the history of Euro-American trading posts in and adjacent to the Navajo Nation, and the notion of “authenticity” as a value marker of Indigenous art and craft. Developed in partnership between the Henry and The Bell Gallery at Brown University, ojo|-|ólǫ́ emerged from Riege’s material research with Navajo collections housed at Brown’s Haffenreffer Museum of Anthropology and the University of Washington’s Burke Museum of Natural History and Culture. The resulting body of work celebrates the ancestral knowledge and labor contained within Indigenous-made objects, while investigating the role of museums and other institutions in the dissemination and dispossession of knowledge about Indigenous cultures. For his tactile and modular soft sculptures, Riege employs exaggerated scale and combines synthetic and natural materials to playfully question ideas of authenticity and Indigenous artistic production. Processes of making and remaking embody a living practice of exchange that draws together webs of connection across time and space. The exhibition also includes objects from the Burke collection displayed alongside assorted ephemera from Riege’s own home and studio. The resulting non-hierarchal display blurs lines between past and present, private and public, and real and fake, animating the dynamism and dimensionality of Indigenous cultures and identities. ojo|-|ólǫ́ invites collective reflection on the practices of institutions that have accumulated Indigenous art, while advancing a call for Indigenous cultural resurgence in the present and toward our shared future.

item.title: Moomins' Sea Adventures & Tove and the Sea
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: National Nordic Museum, 2655 NW Market Street, Seattle, WA

Dive into the whimsical world of Moominvalley and the life of its creator, Tove Jansson, with *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* and *Tove and the Sea*. *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* brings Moominvalley to life with illustrations and life-sized characters from the stories. In the center of the exhibit is an interactive island complete with a lighthouse and rowboats that invites visitors to imagine adventures of their own. *Tove and the Sea* offers a more intimate counterpoint, tracing Tove Jansson’s lifelong relationship with the Finnish archipelago through large-scale photographs and reflective texts.

item.title: Figure/Ground: New Criteria
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Figure/Ground: New Criteria is inspired by cultural historian Sarah Lewis’s concept of “groundwork aesthetics,” exploring figure-ground relationships and their connection to social tension. It builds on Lewis’s work, which—set against the expansion of Stand Your Ground laws across the United States since 2005, laws that broadly define rights to use lethal self-defense against perceived threats—describes a range of artistic interventions that reimagine “the literal and figurative meaning of ground.” Primarily featuring works from the Henry collection created in the twenty-first century, Figure/Ground reflects a period in which hard-won civil rights and claims to self-determination have been eroded across the US, disproportionately affecting Black, Brown, LGBTQ\+, and other marginalized communities.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: Kameelah Janan Rasheed: we leak, we exceed
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Kameelah Janan Rasheed \(b. 1985, East Palo Alto, CA; lives and works in Brooklyn, NY\) is a self-described “learner” who creates multi-media works that explore the poetics-pleasures-politics of Black knowledge production, information technologies, \[un\]learning, and belief formation. She often integrates found images and words alongside her own writing to create collaged wall installations and experimental video works. Across her work, Rasheed investigates the revision and life cycles of text, embracing expanded and embodied forms of writing and reading. we leak, we exceed will activate the unique volume and multiple vantage points of the Henry’s double-height gallery, drawing together threads from physics, Black critical thought, and information theory to create an immersive environment that interrogates the spatial and social implications of compression. A common process used in data storage, spatial organization, and information systems, compression abbreviates and collapses complex ideas into more simplified forms. Rasheed questions the way compression comes at the cost of nuance and creates unrecoverable losses. She draws parallels between the compression of information and the containment of people, both physically and through the structuring and defining of identities. Through a network of video, sound, and architectural mark-making, Rasheed proposes alternatively what she calls “an embrace of Black excess and expansion” as a liberatory practice.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: You've Had It In You All Along
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Avery Barnes Gallery, 120 First Ave. S., Seattle, WA

Artists: Barbara Earl Thomas | Tariqa Waters | Cristina Martinez | Robert Wade | Maikoiyo Alley-Barnes | Nahom Ghirmay “ You’ve Had It In You All Along” is an affirmation of our capability to weather any storm. It serves as a vital reminder that the tools required to reach your destination are already etched within your spirit. We already carry the brilliance and the beauty of what it takes—the confidence, the strength, the security, the poise, and the knowledge to achieve our goals. The grit, the rigor, the radical ideas, and the voice required to transcend the world around us are not external gifts to be acquired; they are inherent powers waiting for the moment of their activation.  In the process of overcoming—when the pathway forward is unclear and the effort feels endless—it is easy to lose sight of our own internal compass. When we stand face-to-face with rejection, heartbreak, or the heavy weight of difficult seasons, this exhibition serves as a sanctuary to remind us that we have always been more than enough, exactly as we are. Breakthroughs happen in the space where we are gentle with ourselves, allowing for the activation of an inner wisdom and intuition that has been present since our first breath.

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

item.title: Moomins' Sea Adventures & Tove and the Sea
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: National Nordic Museum, 2655 NW Market Street, Seattle, WA

Dive into the whimsical world of Moominvalley and the life of its creator, Tove Jansson, with *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* and *Tove and the Sea*. *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* brings Moominvalley to life with illustrations and life-sized characters from the stories. In the center of the exhibit is an interactive island complete with a lighthouse and rowboats that invites visitors to imagine adventures of their own. *Tove and the Sea* offers a more intimate counterpoint, tracing Tove Jansson’s lifelong relationship with the Finnish archipelago through large-scale photographs and reflective texts.

item.title: Figure/Ground: New Criteria
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Figure/Ground: New Criteria is inspired by cultural historian Sarah Lewis’s concept of “groundwork aesthetics,” exploring figure-ground relationships and their connection to social tension. It builds on Lewis’s work, which—set against the expansion of Stand Your Ground laws across the United States since 2005, laws that broadly define rights to use lethal self-defense against perceived threats—describes a range of artistic interventions that reimagine “the literal and figurative meaning of ground.” Primarily featuring works from the Henry collection created in the twenty-first century, Figure/Ground reflects a period in which hard-won civil rights and claims to self-determination have been eroded across the US, disproportionately affecting Black, Brown, LGBTQ\+, and other marginalized communities.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: Kameelah Janan Rasheed: we leak, we exceed
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Kameelah Janan Rasheed \(b. 1985, East Palo Alto, CA; lives and works in Brooklyn, NY\) is a self-described “learner” who creates multi-media works that explore the poetics-pleasures-politics of Black knowledge production, information technologies, \[un\]learning, and belief formation. She often integrates found images and words alongside her own writing to create collaged wall installations and experimental video works. Across her work, Rasheed investigates the revision and life cycles of text, embracing expanded and embodied forms of writing and reading. we leak, we exceed will activate the unique volume and multiple vantage points of the Henry’s double-height gallery, drawing together threads from physics, Black critical thought, and information theory to create an immersive environment that interrogates the spatial and social implications of compression. A common process used in data storage, spatial organization, and information systems, compression abbreviates and collapses complex ideas into more simplified forms. Rasheed questions the way compression comes at the cost of nuance and creates unrecoverable losses. She draws parallels between the compression of information and the containment of people, both physically and through the structuring and defining of identities. Through a network of video, sound, and architectural mark-making, Rasheed proposes alternatively what she calls “an embrace of Black excess and expansion” as a liberatory practice.

item.title: You've Had It In You All Along
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Avery Barnes Gallery, 120 First Ave. S., Seattle, WA

Artists: Barbara Earl Thomas | Tariqa Waters | Cristina Martinez | Robert Wade | Maikoiyo Alley-Barnes | Nahom Ghirmay “ You’ve Had It In You All Along” is an affirmation of our capability to weather any storm. It serves as a vital reminder that the tools required to reach your destination are already etched within your spirit. We already carry the brilliance and the beauty of what it takes—the confidence, the strength, the security, the poise, and the knowledge to achieve our goals. The grit, the rigor, the radical ideas, and the voice required to transcend the world around us are not external gifts to be acquired; they are inherent powers waiting for the moment of their activation.  In the process of overcoming—when the pathway forward is unclear and the effort feels endless—it is easy to lose sight of our own internal compass. When we stand face-to-face with rejection, heartbreak, or the heavy weight of difficult seasons, this exhibition serves as a sanctuary to remind us that we have always been more than enough, exactly as we are. Breakthroughs happen in the space where we are gentle with ourselves, allowing for the activation of an inner wisdom and intuition that has been present since our first breath.

Event details

item.title: Springing Forward: Brunch for Black Economic Brilliance
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Amazon Frontier, 2205 7th Ave, Seattle, WA

Experience the Central District Community Preservation and Development Authority \(CDCPDA\) benefit brunch: Springing Forward: Brunch for Black Economic Brilliance. Now in its second year, this annual gathering brings together more than 200 visionaries—professionals, philanthropists, policymakers, and community leaders—united by a shared commitment to advancing Black economic empowerment in Seattle’s Central District and beyond.  Anchored in the themes: Collaborate. Sustain. Thrive. The brunch creates a dynamic space for meaningful connection, shared learning, and forward-looking action. The program will highlight current tenants, key partnerships, and community activations, while also providing important updates on the progress of the McKinney Center for Community and Economic Development—and a look ahead at what’s to come for the future of this vital project. Guests will engage directly with CDCPDA staff and board members, as well as tenants of the McKinney Center, a cornerstone community hub driving inclusive economic growth. In partnership with Amazon’s Black Employee Network, this event moves beyond celebration to the intentional cultivation of Black excellence and long-term community sustainability. Join us for a meaningful experience that goes beyond traditional networking—one rooted in collaboration, resilience, and collective progress. Your presence helps fuel transformative impact and strengthens the foundation for a thriving, equitable future. Attendee | $150.00﻿ ﻿Full access to the brunch, keynote, and networking. Your ticket directly supports the McKinney Center and community-rooted development in the Central District. Vendor Table | $175.00 Table your business or organization during the first hour of the event. Includes one attendee ticket. Full Table \(8 seats\) | $1,200.00 Bring your team or clients. Includes 8 individual tickets — a savings of $1,000 vs. individual pricing. Guest details can be submitted after purchase. Be part of the movement. Together, we spring forward—collaborating, sustaining, and thriving. Our Emcee TraeAnna Holiday, a true Seattleite, has watched her city change in many ways. She started training in media from the young age of 9 and continued through school up to Howard University and University of Washington. Through studying abroad multiple times, gaining her degree in Communications and Urban Studies, and being displaced by gentrification, she's now fueled to tell her neighborhood's stories through film and media. She found a home for this work at Converge Media as a two-time Emmy award winning Media Personality, TV Show Host and Producer, with her daily talk show, The Day With Trae, and other productions. As Communications Director for Washington state’s Office of Equity, she is able to bring her knowledge and enthusiasm for communications and the community to the state level. Flourishing with her style, passion, and drive, she dynamically creates impact media throughout her work, and helps the next generation find their voice in the world of media.  Our Keynote Speaker LaNesha McCoy is a skilled public historian with over 20 years experience leading African American museums and history initiatives. She is Executive Director of BlackPast.org, the world’s largest online encyclopedia on Black history which was founded by Dr. Quintard Taylor. Prior, she served as President & CEO of the Northwest African American Museum from 2017 to 2023 where she led strategic planning, recordsetting fundraising, and innovative programming. LaNesha is Past National President of the Association of African American Museums Board of Directors, only the sixth woman in history to serve in that capacity. A member of the International Council of Museums, she has studied museums globally in Kenya, Ghana, South Africa, England, Germany, and Israel. An ordained clergy, LaNesha is Assistant Pastor at the historic New Hope Missionary Baptist Church in Seattle's Central District and Executive Administrator of the New Hope Community Development Institute, which is focused on building affordable housing for people of color. Never forgetting her roots or the many mentors that shaped her life, she now has three master's degrees and is currently a PhD Candidate at the University of Washington where she teaches and researches African American educational history. She's a mentor and advocate, leading initiatives to support education.

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Saturday, April 18, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

item.title: Green Issaquah: Restoration Event at Park Pointe
item.description:
Saturday, April 18, 2026 at 9 AM

Location: SE Evans St, Issaquah, WA

Join the Issaquah Alps Trails Club and Green Issaquah on the third Saturday of each month for invasive species removal events at Park Pointe! We will be removing invasive species such as Himalayan blackberry bushes to prepare for our fall planting event. These invasive plants inhibit the growth of native plants, and removing them will ensure the longevity and health of our newest additions. This event is part of our ongoing efforts to help meet the Issaquah Climate Action Plan goal of 55% tree canopy cover by 2035. When and Where Date & Time: Saturday, April 18th, 2025, 9 AM - Noon Where: Park Pointe Where to Park Parking for Park Pointe is available along SE Evans Street, which is overflow for Issaquah High School and the parking for the Sportsman Club. Where to Meet We will be meeting at the end of the dirt road at the trailhead. What to Bring Bring your own gloves if you have them. There will be gloves available for those who don't have their own. Please dress in layers and in clothing you don't mind getting dirty. Closed-toed shoes are required. Long sleeves and pants, especially denim, are strongly recommended. Bring a full water bottle and snacks. Don't forget to bring your sunscreen! Point of Contact Anne Newcomb \(anne@annenewcomb.net\) and Matt LaBelle \(mattlabelle@hotmail.com\), your work party leaders.

item.title: Living with Conviction: Sentenced to Debt for Life in Washington State Exhibit
item.description:
Saturday, April 18, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Northwest Worklofts (Commons), 107 West Denny Way, Seattle, WA

Saturday, April 18, 2026, 10am - 6pm This exhibitconfronts how Washington courts have been sentencing people not only to prison but also to a lifetime of debt. Location Link: Central Library Room Location: Level 8 - Gallery Registration Required: Registration is not required. Detailed Description: "Living with Conviction: Sentenced to Debt for Life in Washington State" confronts how Washington courts have been sentencing people not only to prison but also to a lifetime of debt. The project leverages multimedia storytelling and community conversations to raise awareness about and advocate for an end to crippling court-imposed costs, fees, fines, and restitution, commonly referred to as Legal Financial Obligations, or LFOs. "Living with Conviction: Sentenced to Debt for Life in Washington State," goes beyond polarizing headlines and statistics, shining a light on this injustice by sharing stories of our common humanity., Thanks to our partner Living with Conviction ., Thanks to our sponsor The Seattle Public Library Foundation . Event Logistics: The gallery is open during Central Libray's open hours., Living with Conviction is a 501\(c\)\(3\) nonprofit that works to secure economic and racial justice with and by marginalized communities, especially formerly incarcerated, through community-driven research, storytelling, and legal empowerment strategies., This exhibit runs through July 26, 2026, Learn more about Exhibits . item.title: Eric-Paul Riege: ojo|-|ólǫ́
item.description:
Saturday, April 18, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

ojo|-|ólǫ́ is an exhibition of recent and newly commissioned work by Diné artist Eric-Paul Riege \(b. 1994, Na’nízhoozhí \[Gallup, New Mexico\]\) that includes sculpture, textile, collage, and video, activated by moments of performance. Across this work, Riege combines customary Diné practices of weaving, silversmithing, and beading with contemporary cultural forms, exploring Diné cosmology, the history of Euro-American trading posts in and adjacent to the Navajo Nation, and the notion of “authenticity” as a value marker of Indigenous art and craft. Developed in partnership between the Henry and The Bell Gallery at Brown University, ojo|-|ólǫ́ emerged from Riege’s material research with Navajo collections housed at Brown’s Haffenreffer Museum of Anthropology and the University of Washington’s Burke Museum of Natural History and Culture. The resulting body of work celebrates the ancestral knowledge and labor contained within Indigenous-made objects, while investigating the role of museums and other institutions in the dissemination and dispossession of knowledge about Indigenous cultures. For his tactile and modular soft sculptures, Riege employs exaggerated scale and combines synthetic and natural materials to playfully question ideas of authenticity and Indigenous artistic production. Processes of making and remaking embody a living practice of exchange that draws together webs of connection across time and space. The exhibition also includes objects from the Burke collection displayed alongside assorted ephemera from Riege’s own home and studio. The resulting non-hierarchal display blurs lines between past and present, private and public, and real and fake, animating the dynamism and dimensionality of Indigenous cultures and identities. ojo|-|ólǫ́ invites collective reflection on the practices of institutions that have accumulated Indigenous art, while advancing a call for Indigenous cultural resurgence in the present and toward our shared future.

Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

item.title: Living with Conviction: Sentenced to Debt for Life in Washington State Exhibit
item.description:
Sunday, April 19, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Northwest Worklofts (Commons), 107 West Denny Way, Seattle, WA

Sunday, April 19, 2026, 10am - 6pm This exhibitconfronts how Washington courts have been sentencing people not only to prison but also to a lifetime of debt. Location Link: Central Library Room Location: Level 8 - Gallery Registration Required: Registration is not required. Detailed Description: "Living with Conviction: Sentenced to Debt for Life in Washington State" confronts how Washington courts have been sentencing people not only to prison but also to a lifetime of debt. The project leverages multimedia storytelling and community conversations to raise awareness about and advocate for an end to crippling court-imposed costs, fees, fines, and restitution, commonly referred to as Legal Financial Obligations, or LFOs. "Living with Conviction: Sentenced to Debt for Life in Washington State," goes beyond polarizing headlines and statistics, shining a light on this injustice by sharing stories of our common humanity., Thanks to our partner Living with Conviction ., Thanks to our sponsor The Seattle Public Library Foundation . Event Logistics: The gallery is open during Central Libray's open hours., Living with Conviction is a 501\(c\)\(3\) nonprofit that works to secure economic and racial justice with and by marginalized communities, especially formerly incarcerated, through community-driven research, storytelling, and legal empowerment strategies., This exhibit runs through July 26, 2026, Learn more about Exhibits . Contact Us: If you have questions about this event, Ask Us or call 206-386-4636. ADA Accommodations: We can provide accommodations for people with disabilities at Library events. Please contactLEAP at least seven days before the event to request accommodations., All Library programs are free and open to the public.

Event details

item.title: Eric-Paul Riege: ojo|-|ólǫ́
item.description:
Sunday, April 19, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

ojo|-|ólǫ́ is an exhibition of recent and newly commissioned work by Diné artist Eric-Paul Riege \(b. 1994, Na’nízhoozhí \[Gallup, New Mexico\]\) that includes sculpture, textile, collage, and video, activated by moments of performance. Across this work, Riege combines customary Diné practices of weaving, silversmithing, and beading with contemporary cultural forms, exploring Diné cosmology, the history of Euro-American trading posts in and adjacent to the Navajo Nation, and the notion of “authenticity” as a value marker of Indigenous art and craft. Developed in partnership between the Henry and The Bell Gallery at Brown University, ojo|-|ólǫ́ emerged from Riege’s material research with Navajo collections housed at Brown’s Haffenreffer Museum of Anthropology and the University of Washington’s Burke Museum of Natural History and Culture. The resulting body of work celebrates the ancestral knowledge and labor contained within Indigenous-made objects, while investigating the role of museums and other institutions in the dissemination and dispossession of knowledge about Indigenous cultures. For his tactile and modular soft sculptures, Riege employs exaggerated scale and combines synthetic and natural materials to playfully question ideas of authenticity and Indigenous artistic production. Processes of making and remaking embody a living practice of exchange that draws together webs of connection across time and space. The exhibition also includes objects from the Burke collection displayed alongside assorted ephemera from Riege’s own home and studio. The resulting non-hierarchal display blurs lines between past and present, private and public, and real and fake, animating the dynamism and dimensionality of Indigenous cultures and identities. ojo|-|ólǫ́ invites collective reflection on the practices of institutions that have accumulated Indigenous art, while advancing a call for Indigenous cultural resurgence in the present and toward our shared future.

item.title: Moomins' Sea Adventures & Tove and the Sea
item.description:
Sunday, April 19, 2026 at 10 AM

In the center of the exhibit is an interactive island complete with a lighthouse and rowboats that invites visitors to imagine adventures of their own. *Tove and the Sea* offers a more intimate counterpoint, tracing Tove Jansson’s lifelong relationship with the Finnish archipelago through large-scale photographs and reflective texts.

item.title: Figure/Ground: New Criteria
item.description:
Sunday, April 19, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Figure/Ground: New Criteria is inspired by cultural historian Sarah Lewis’s concept of “groundwork aesthetics,” exploring figure-ground relationships and their connection to social tension. It builds on Lewis’s work, which—set against the expansion of Stand Your Ground laws across the United States since 2005, laws that broadly define rights to use lethal self-defense against perceived threats—describes a range of artistic interventions that reimagine “the literal and figurative meaning of ground.” Primarily featuring works from the Henry collection created in the twenty-first century, Figure/Ground reflects a period in which hard-won civil rights and claims to self-determination have been eroded across the US, disproportionately affecting Black, Brown, LGBTQ\+, and other marginalized communities.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Sunday, April 19, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Sunday, April 19, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Sunday, April 19, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Sunday, April 19, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: Kameelah Janan Rasheed: we leak, we exceed
item.description:
Sunday, April 19, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Kameelah Janan Rasheed \(b. 1985, East Palo Alto, CA; lives and works in Brooklyn, NY\) is a self-described “learner” who creates multi-media works that explore the poetics-pleasures-politics of Black knowledge production, information technologies, \[un\]learning, and belief formation. She often integrates found images and words alongside her own writing to create collaged wall installations and experimental video works. Across her work, Rasheed investigates the revision and life cycles of text, embracing expanded and embodied forms of writing and reading. we leak, we exceed will activate the unique volume and multiple vantage points of the Henry’s double-height gallery, drawing together threads from physics, Black critical thought, and information theory to create an immersive environment that interrogates the spatial and social implications of compression. A common process used in data storage, spatial organization, and information systems, compression abbreviates and collapses complex ideas into more simplified forms. Rasheed questions the way compression comes at the cost of nuance and creates unrecoverable losses. She draws parallels between the compression of information and the containment of people, both physically and through the structuring and defining of identities. Through a network of video, sound, and architectural mark-making, Rasheed proposes alternatively what she calls “an embrace of Black excess and expansion” as a liberatory practice.

item.title: Ragamala Indian Classical Music and Arts Celebration
item.description:
Sunday, April 19, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Olympic Sculpture Park, 2901 Western Ave, Seattle, WA

Join Ragamala Seattle for a family-friendly day of Indian music and art activities at the Seattle Asian Art Museum. 11 am-noon Enjoy the beats and rhythms of Indian classical music by participating in a drum circle led by Ravi Albright of the Seattle Tabla Institute. noon-1 pm Learn to sing Hindustani vocal music in this beginner-friendly workshop led by teaching artists Suchitra Iyer and Anindita Das 2-4 pm Enjoy a concert and performance from celebrated Sitar player Pt. Kushal Das accompanied by Shri. Sandip Ghosh on Tabla. Pt. Kushal Das, renowned Sitar and Surbahar artist, was born in a musical family in Kolkata. His father Sailen Das was a disciple of Lakshman Bhattacharya and Pt. Ravi Shankar. His uncle Santanu Das was a disciple of Pt. Ali Akbar Khan. He started his training under the guidance of his father and uncle. Later he received training from Sanjoy Bandopadhyay and from Ajoy Sinha Roy \(a disciple of Pt. Allauddin Khan\) and took vocal lessons from Pt. Manas Chakraborty and Pt. Ramkrishna Basu. Pt. Das will be accompanied on the tabla by Sandip Ghosh, who has been a disciple of Pt. Anindo Chatterjee for the last 18 years. Sandip has accompanied distinguished artists like Pt. Ajoy Chakrabarty, Ud. Shahid Parvez, Ud. Shujaat Khan, Pt. Hari Prasad Chaurasia and Vid. Kaushiki Chakrabarty. He is also a solo artist, collaborating with bands worldwide.

Event details

item.title: You've Had It In You All Along
item.description:
Sunday, April 19, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Avery Barnes Gallery, 120 First Ave. S., Seattle, WA

Artists: Barbara Earl Thomas | Tariqa Waters | Cristina Martinez | Robert Wade | Maikoiyo Alley-Barnes | Nahom Ghirmay “ You’ve Had It In You All Along” is an affirmation of our capability to weather any storm. It serves as a vital reminder that the tools required to reach your destination are already etched within your spirit. We already carry the brilliance and the beauty of what it takes—the confidence, the strength, the security, the poise, and the knowledge to achieve our goals. The grit, the rigor, the radical ideas, and the voice required to transcend the world around us are not external gifts to be acquired; they are inherent powers waiting for the moment of their activation.  In the process of overcoming—when the pathway forward is unclear and the effort feels endless—it is easy to lose sight of our own internal compass. When we stand face-to-face with rejection, heartbreak, or the heavy weight of difficult seasons, this exhibition serves as a sanctuary to remind us that we have always been more than enough, exactly as we are. Breakthroughs happen in the space where we are gentle with ourselves, allowing for the activation of an inner wisdom and intuition that has been present since our first breath.
